title: Cloudy or Milky Cat Urine: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Discover why your cat has cloudy or milky urine, the urgency of the situation, and how a photo can help your veterinarian with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Cloudy cat urine indicates potential inflammation or infection and requires a veterinary exam within 48 hours, though immediate emergency care is needed if your cat is straining or unable to urinate.

What does it mean if my cat's urine is cloudy or milky?

Normal cat urine should be clear with a pale yellow to amber hue. When urine appears cloudy, milky, or turbid, it typically indicates the presence of particles such as:

  • White blood cells (pus)
  • Bacteria
  • Microscopic crystals
  • Excess protein

This change is a clinical sign of inflammation, infection, or a metabolic issue within the urinary tract.

Is cloudy cat urine a veterinary emergency?

While cloudy urine alone is usually a low-priority concern, you should take the following actions:

  • Schedule a veterinary consultation within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from worsening.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your cat is straining to urinate or unable to pass urine.
  • Watch for lethargy or vomiting, as these can be signs of a life-threatening urinary blockage.

How can taking a photo of the urine help with my cat's triage?

Providing your veterinarian with a clear photograph of the urine is an invaluable diagnostic tool that allows the team to:

  • Assess the degree of opacity and look for visible sediment.
  • Identify any discoloration that may have settled.
  • Prioritize your cat’s appointment based on the visual evidence.
  • Establish a baseline for comparison during the physical exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cloudy or milky urine in cats can be indicative of cystitis, potentially caused by increased numbers of white blood cells, red blood cells, and/or bacteria. However, the absence of visible bacteria in urine sediment does not rule out a urinary tract infection. If clinical signs and urinalysis suggest infection, a urine culture and antimicrobial susceptibility test are recommended. It is important to differentiate true bacteriuria from stain precipitate by filtering the stain or evaluating unstained sediment. Urolithiasis and matrix-crystalline plugs, commonly found in the urethra, can also contribute to changes in urine appearance. A complete urinalysis, including assessment of color, turbidity, and microscopic examination of sediment, is crucial for proper diagnosis, along with considering potential pre-analytical artifacts related to urine sample handling.

Chapter: Urology, Clinical Pathology, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1494)

faqs.question: Can a urinary tract infection (UTI) cause cloudy urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, UTIs are a common cause of cloudy urine in cats because the immune system sends white blood cells to the bladder to fight the infection, resulting in a milky appearance.

faqs.question: What are urinary crystals?
faqs.answer: Urinary crystals are microscopic mineral formations that can make urine look cloudy or gritty. If left untreated, these crystals can clump together to form bladder stones.

faqs.question: How should I collect a urine sample for the vet?
faqs.answer: You can use non-absorbent litter provided by your vet or try to catch a mid-stream sample in a clean container. Always call your clinic first to see if they prefer to collect a sterile sample via cystocentesis.
